Please describe your research career to date
Building on 12-years of research, clinical, and leadership experience, I am ready to accelerate development as a
clinical academic and future leader through this fellowship. This fellowship will support my progression towards my
goal of becoming a post-doctoral clinical academic practitioner in Urgent and Emergency Care (UEC) with a
physiotherapy background.
Research and academic experience:
My passion for research began with my undergraduate dissertation and has continued throughout my career. As a
newly qualified physiotherapist, I identified a gap in both my role and job satisfaction. This prompted me to seek
research opportunities, leading to appointment as project manager for NIHR NWL Collaboration for Leadership in
Applied Health Research (CLAHRC). In this role, I led a year-long quality improvement (QI) project on non-
invasive ventilation and an interventional trial on novel monitoring techniques for acute respiratory failure.
On return to full-time clinical practice in UEC, I continued developing my research interests. In 2020, I submitted
an NIHR PCAF application focusing on UEC workforce, assessed as ‘fundable but not funded,’ with feedback
highlighting my strong commitment to a clinical academic career. Although unsuccessful, I continued pursuing
research development alongside my clinical role by completing a PGCert in Research Methods. I progressed this
to complete an MRes (2024), exploring impact of enhanced and advanced practice in UEC. My research interests
in UEC workforce align with my advancing practice role and national priorities for UEC transformation, alongside
my MRes study topic.
To build this topic toward a DCAF I was successfully awarded a pre-doctoral bridging fellowship, supporting
development of this application. This fellowship provided a platform to advance my skills and knowledge as an
aspiring clinical academic and supported identified learning gaps including research/grant funding. The scheme
has enhanced my awareness of supervisory relationships, including understanding supervisory roles
and complementarity of expertise in developing strong research applications. It also allowed me to build my
supervisory and mentorship team for the DCAF.
I have actively pursued workshops, webinars, and academic training relevant to my clinical setting, alongside
membership in the NIHR Emergency Care Incubator, and participation in an RSS-led fellowship application day
and NIHR PI essentials training. I have led service evaluations and audits, including recent projects on ankle
sprain and ambulance access to UEC. To broaden my clinical studies experience, I was associate Principal
Investigator for a national study in management of fractures (DRAFT-CASP) and am currently Principal
Investigator for a national study in cellulitis management. I am contributing to an expert panel on an NIHR-funded
study exploring UEC non-medical practitioner roles (SkillMix-ED NIHR131356). I am a co-applicant on a Royal
College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) grant-funded study on UEC consultant-level practice roles, which further
inspired my DCAF project. I was appointed as a research fellow one-day/week on this study supporting initial grant
writing, leading a scoping review around UEC workforce roles, and disseminating findings. This dual role as
clinician and research fellow has proved pivotal in immersing myself in the field of UEC clinical academia.
My career experiences include but are not limited to curation, analysis, methodology and project administration,
alongside sound research governance. My outputs include publications in peer-reviewed journals, conference
presentations, and NICE best practice and British Thoracic Society guidance contributions.

Profession and clinical experience:
Over 12-years, my career has advanced into a senior leadership role in a large UEC multi-professional team. As a
respected member of the leadership team, I lead service improvement projects, develop clinical guidelines, and
supervise early career colleagues, while strengthening my expertise in advanced clinical practice. As the first
physiotherapist in a clinical leadership role within my UEC department I’ve pioneered integration of physiotherapy
practitioners into the team, advocating for the discipline through recruitment, pathway development, and advancing
autonomy. I’ve shared this work through networks, for example Chartered Society of Physiotherapy (CSP) and
Advanced Practice Physiotherapy Network (APPN).
Education and teaching development:
I actively seek opportunities to share knowledge, supervising staff and students while managing twenty-one
practitioners. In addition to internal teaching, I contribute to undergraduate and postgraduate education and
contribute as an OSCE invigilator for advanced practice modules. I was a teaching fellow in advanced assessment
at Surrey University (2021-2022). My interest in QI led me to complete a QI coaching programme (2017), enabling
me to coach others through QI projects.
Research leadership:
My research leadership skills, particularly in governance, PPIE, and study setup, significantly developed during my
CLAHRC role and have continued to grow in my current research fellow position. Leadership courses for my
clinical role have provided transferrable skills for academic leadership, enabling me to supervise research nurses
in publication writing. I maintain Good Clinical Practice compliance and have completed introductory PPIE training
to enhance my involvement in a hospital patient engagement group.
Career trajectory:
I have advanced in my clinical role and am a respected leader within my specialty, continuously expanding my
knowledge and skills to deliver high-quality care. I am eager to advance my clinical academic career through the
NIHR schemes, with the DCAF as the next step. I aim to mentor and supervise UEC staff, role modelling research
development, and collaborate with service-users to strengthen existing research culture in UEC. I aim to
strengthen my relationship with the partner HEI for an honorary contract and use this fellowship to explore post-
doctoral opportunities with NIHR, advancing my career and networking within the clinical academic community.

APPLICATION DETAILS.Plain English Summary of Research:
Written with support and review of the NIHR Research Support Service PPIE team
A study to find out how consultant nurse and consultant practitioner roles are started and then supported in urgent
and emergency care (UEC) services so that a toolkit can be produced together, by staff, researchers and patients,
to improve services in the future.
Background:
UEC includes NHS services for both life-threatening and non-life-threatening injuries and illnesses. These services
are provided in an Emergency Department (ED) or Urgent Treatment Centre. As the UK population grows and
lives longer, more people need to use these services. This means we need to think about the people who work
there, and make sure they have the right skills to look after the population.
Consultant practitioners (CP) include highly experienced skilled nurses or allied health professionals. Each ED is
meant to have at least one CP, however a recent survey found this was not the case, and we don’t know why. This
means not everyone may get the same care, with some EDs having more senior staff than others. Across the
healthcare system CPs have been good, helping to make patients better and happier when unwell.
What do we want to do?
To find out:
1. What it’s like to be a CP, especially when starting as a CP, how they make a difference in UK UEC settings, as
well as to find out from service-users and NHS employers.
2. What CPs think about the good and bad of starting in this role, if they feel looked after and prepared in the role,
and what they would like done differently.
3. If patients like having CPs and if they think they are helpful or what would be helpful for CP roles of the future.
Together with patients and CPs, we want to create a toolkit to find out how to get CPs into all UEC services, and
then look at how much good they are doing, or they can do to improve services in the future.
How are we going to do it:
There will be four parts:
1. We will look at published research on this topic
2. We will interview CPs, NHS staff who employ CPs and individuals working in integrated care boards who
help put together workforce strategies across the UK
3. We will have group discussions with NHS staff in CP roles in UEC and patients/service-users who have
recently accessed UEC across the UK
4. After this we will run a workshop to co-produce a toolkit to help bring in and see how good CPs are, ready
to be tested in the workplace.
Public involvement and engagement:
Patients/service-users who recently accessed UEC gave their thoughts and ideas; they felt this was an important
research idea because it is hard working in UEC, and there’s not always enough or the right people to help
patients.
A study patient and public advisory group (PAG) will be set up. Group members will be involved in developing
materials for getting people involved in the study and coming up with questions for interviews and group
discussions. They will help look at findings, put together workshop documents and share all study findings with the
right people.
The group will meet three times in the study, and in between, researchers will share updates through email or
letters.
Sharing the findings:
We will share findings in research journals, talk at local and national conferences. We will also talk about our
findings with policymakers and professional bodies so together we can come up with the next steps, as well as test
our new toolkit.
APPLICATION DETAILS.Scientific Abstract:
Background:
Urgent and emergency care (UEC) services are in crisis with increased demand, overcrowding and long waits
placing stress onto the UEC workforce. The 2024 Darzi report identified the need for new multidisciplinary care
models, with workforce growth and retention as a priority. There is evidence that poor senior clinical leadership
results in worse care outcomes, quality, safety, and effectiveness of services. Senior clinical leadership can be
provided by consultant practitioner roles (CP) with national workforce standards recommending at least one CP in
each Emergency Department (ED). A recent survey of CP roles in the UK showed this standard was not being
met, with currently only 37 ED CPs known across the UK. Consultant-level practice builds on advancing practice,
offering benefits of improved patient outcomes, career progression, staff retention and stronger clinical leadership.
However, reasons for the lack of CP role implementation in EDs is not known, and the impact these roles have on
patients, individual EDs and the wider system has not been explored.
Aims and Objectives:
To co-produce a toolkit for the introduction, implementation and impact of consultant-level practice roles in UEC
This study will:
1. Review current literature surrounding consultant-level practice roles through qualitative systematic review with
meta-synthesis
2. Explore experiences and perceptions of current CPs in UK UEC settings regarding role transition,
preparedness, implementation, impact and effectiveness
3. Determine patient/service-user experiences and perceptions of CP roles in UK UEC settings, and priorities for
measuring impact, effectiveness and evaluation
4. Explore macro and meso-level stakeholder perspectives of the CP role in UK UEC settings to identify
implementation, impact and effectiveness metrics
Methods: The project has four inter-related work-packages (WP).
WP1: qualitative systematic review with meta-synthesis
A systematic review will be conducted to explore literature on CP role implementation, effectiveness, evaluation
and impact. This will include search strategies, quality appraisal, and meta-synthesis using a synthesis matrix.
WP2: Semi-structured interviews and WP3: Focus Groups
WP2 and WP3 focus on gathering opinions and experiences of stakeholders at macro (policy), meso
(organisational), and micro (individual) levels.
WP4: Implementation and impact toolkit development (informed by previous work packages)
A stakeholder workshop (12-15 participants) will co-design a implementation and impact toolkit for acceptability
testing, using realist evaluation principles. The toolkit will target macro and meso groups and those pursuing future
UEC CP roles, ultimately benefiting UEC service-users.
Impact:
This study will achieve impact by developing a toolkit that supports sustainable workforce planning in the NHS at
consultant-practice level. We anticipate this toolkit will be transferable to other areas and will be available under an
open license agreement for further validation and use.
Dissemination:
Key outcomes include four planned deliverables, with dissemination through Patient and Public Advisory Group
(PAG) collaboration, conference presentations, at least three publications, and increased service-user awareness
of CP roles in UEC.
APPLICATION DETAILS.Detailed Research Plan:
Problem being addressed:
In 2024, Health Services Research UK launched a call for action setting out the urgent need for research and
evidence to support decision-making in designing the healthcare workforce. This report outlines the current
challenged state of the workforce, highlighting the need for rigorous evidence to improve policy and practice in
timely and actionable forms.
Urgent and emergency care (UEC) services face a crisis, with a need for evidence on managing workload,
maintaining care quality, and ensuring a positive service-user experience. The workforce is the NHS' most
valuable and costly resource, so evidence-driven workforce decisions are crucial for service, staff, and service-
user outcomes. “The UK spends about 65% of the NHS budget on its workforce, yet it invests a tiny fraction of the
public and government budget for health and care research on understanding that workforce and improving its
efficiency, effectiveness, and outcomes” (Walsh et al 2024).
The Darzi report (2024) highlights gaps in the experienced UEC workforce, stressing the need for new
multidisciplinary care models to meet the needs of changing UEC demand and scope. This aligns with the 2023
UEC Recovery Plan, which prioritises workforce growth and the need for strong local leadership to drive
transformation. Effective use of multidisciplinary team members is crucial, including individuals from nursing and
AHP clinical backgrounds, alongside developing consultant-level practice roles (NHSE 2023). To address
unnecessary variation, workforce planning must take a strategic approach, aligning with local needs and be based
on sustainable models. One proposed solution in addressing requirements for a senior workforce and supporting
skills shortages has been the development of consultant-level practice roles. Consultant-level practice
encompasses expertise across four domains necessary for enabling quality care, providing a key skill set for
systems leadership and transformation through clinical credibility within a field and adoption of clinical academic
roles as embedded researchers (NHSE 2023). Consultant-level practice roles now extended to nurses,
paramedics, and physiotherapists, are clinical experts responsible for leadership in policy, practice, research,
education, and advancing clinical practice (RCEM/RCN 2020).
There is national demand for consultant-level practice roles to meet complex health needs and improve healthcare
quality, efficiency, and sustainability. Despite the policy direction for these roles there is limited evidence on their
impact on patients, services or the wider health system. This has led to inconsistent implementation and more
recently, challenges from other professions around their effectiveness and scope of practice. Unaddressed issues
in role implementation, impact, and evaluation could lead to health inequalities, poor patient experiences, and
reduced cost-effectiveness.
Rising attendances and rapidly changing care models are increasing workloads, impacting recruitment and
retention in UEC, and highlighting the need to expand CP roles in this area (RCEM 2015). EDs face growing
pressures from increasing patient numbers, ageing populations, and complex conditions, compounded by
economic constraints and higher societal expectations (NHSE 2019, RCEM 2023). The professional Royal
Colleges of Emergency Medicine and Nursing make workforce guidance recommendation of one CP per ED
(RCEM 2020). UEC workforce role development and retention were recently highlighted as research priorities in a
James Lind Alliance research priortisation partnership in emergency care (2022). We are focusing on the role of
the CP in UEC as it is the only specialty area where a defined workforce standard for these posts exists.
Clear role definitions and theory-based evaluations are essential for successful implementation of new roles to
ensure high-quality, cost-effective healthcare delivery. There is a renewed interest in consultant-level practice
roles to improve patient access to emergency care but there is no existing evidence on how these roles can be
successfully implemented in UEC settings, nor are there tools to determine their impact to ensure effective and
equitable access for service-users.
This project provides an opportunity to develop an implementation and impact toolkit for consultant-level practice
contextualised to UEC.
Why is this research important?
Whilst multiple workforce strategies have been released across the UK, evidence is needed to support and
evaluate recommendations. Policymakers are hampered by the lack of research to inform strategic workforce
planning particularly regarding skill and leadership required for the future workforce (HSRUK 2024). Consultant-
level practice career pathways could support professional development while benefiting Trusts through the direct
and indirect impact on patients and services. Without understanding the implementation, impact, and barriers to
these roles, there is a risk of vacancies or role removal. Failure to evaluate and communicate their unique
functions and impact could hinder successful deployment (Fairley 2004).
Achieving people-centered healthcare, a key WHO strategy, requires fundamental shifts in service organisation
(Manley et al 2022). Historically, the Department of Health (1999) recognised consultant-level practice roles as
vital for quality care, health promotion, and addressing inequalities; all critical for patient satisfaction (Crouch et al
2024). Consultant-level practice can be seen as central to transforming service delivery and better meeting
population health needs (NHSE 2017). Effective care models ensure timely, appropriate care from the right
providers (Horvath et al 2023). However, there is limited contemporary evidence on the implementation and impact
of such roles in UEC settings specifically, and stakeholder perceptions of the role are unknown.
A gap exists between demand and recruitment in emergency care. Research priorities from the James Lind
Alliance aligns with the DHSE and NIHR research focus on shaping the future workforce (2024). Optimising CP
roles is essential for health service redesign and sustainability; without evidence, the role risks becoming
undervalued and ineffective patient care being provided.
Review of existing evidence:
There is currently a lack of literature exploring the contemporary nature of consultant-level practice considering the
development of professional roles and health services since their inception more than 25 years ago.
Policy context
Underpinning policy, the NHSE Centre for Advancing Practice released a framework for consultant-level practice,
emphasising expert practice, strategic leadership, system-wide learning, and research (NHSE 2023). The
framework identifies the potential for these roles to have impact but lacks guidance on role introduction,
implementation, and organisational readiness.
NHS strategy highlights the need for person-centered, safe and effective services, which depend on retaining,
growing, and developing the workforce. Rising demand for NHS services is straining the system, raising concerns
about sustaining quality care. NHS policies focus on workforce transformation to improve service and care
standards (Fothergill et al 2022), but workforce shortages affect patient experience, service capacity, and care
transformation (NHSE 2023). UEC is at the forefront of recognising the value of a multi-professional workforce for
high-quality care (Crouch and Brown 2018). Optimal staffing is key to timely, high-quality care and building a
future-ready workforce (Drennan et al 2024) and recent NHS People Plan policies commit to utilising the full range
of skills and experiences for new ways of working (Mann et al 2023).
Workforce development is key to system reform, with CPs essential for leading change and providing expert care.
Clearly defined healthcare roles are needed for service-users to understand who leads, delivers, and evaluates
care in collaboration with them (Crouch et al 2024). This variability is shown to hinder effective workforce planning
(Evans et al 2021). Literature highlights a gap for the need to evaluate implementation and impact to solidify roles
and ensure sustainable contributions to patient care (Gerrish et al 2011, Chan et al 2014), especially given
variation across organisations despite shared national goals. NHS initiatives like High Quality Care for All and
Equity and Excellence stress the importance of patient-centered care and understanding patient perceptions for
sustainable change, goals that CPs could help achieve when embedded.
Organisational support is key for CPs to reach their full potential (Currie 2007), and literature subjectively
highlights the potential benefits of CPs but there are gaps in evidence on role integration and system-wide
workforce planning (Hourahane et al 2012, Kennedy et al 2012). With safe and effective emergency care key NHS
and patient priority, novel solutions are needed to address workforce capacity, and growing staff pressures to
ensure experienced senior clinical staff are retained (NHSE 2017). Successful implementation of national
workforce policies encouraging increasing numbers of roles and from broader professions, requires attention to all
levels of the health system (Taylor et al 2022). However, there is a lack of evidence regarding uptake or what is
required across levels to support system-wide growth of CP roles.
Consultant Practitioner roles
UK consultant nurse roles were introduced in 1999 to support government strategic objectives on nurses'
contribution to the workforce (DofH 1999, Rosser et al 2017). The role contributes to the NHS five-year forward
view (2014, 2017) by supporting new career opportunities, retaining experienced staff and strengthening
leadership. Additional benefits are seen as supporting innovation and strategic direction and enhancing services
for patients (Crouch et al 2024). The development of the role is in response to important challenges facing
healthcare systems including changing health needs of the population and rising public expectations of healthcare
(Kennedy et al 2012).
Consultant-level practice roles offer career progression by expanding practice, enhancing efficiency, and fostering
collaboration. A focused review of the literature in preparing this application found research in this area broadly
explores how CP roles have evolved to meet healthcare demand, and descriptions of workforce models. Literature
identifies the potential of the CP role in improving care through timely access to services, mentorship for early
career staff, strategic leadership, driving innovations and better patient outcomes (Kuczawski et al 2024, Dawood
& Crouch 2018, Manley et al 2024). Studies suggest CPs positively impact patients' wellbeing and quality of life
(Gerrish et al 2011) with greater awareness of the roles' benefits being essential for sustainable workforce
planning and provision of support for effective embedment (Woodward et al 2006, RCEM 2015); however, the
empirical evidence in this area is lacking.
Evidence highlights role importance in strategic influence, partnership working and embracing new practices.
There is need for ongoing support from healthcare managers for continuous role development and exploration of
impacts of the lack of systematic planning or policy can cause when creating a supportive workplace (Humphreys
et al 2007). Organisational support is seen as crucial to maximising benefits of consultant-level practice
(Woodward et al 2006), though how this is implemented in practice remains unclear. Research highlights the need
to prioritise the development of consultant-level roles to support patient-centered care and align with national
health priorities, such as delivering a cost-effective service that meets NHS goals (Jones 2002). A key barrier to
role implementation is the failure to clearly define the unique functions and impact of these roles on health
outcomes (Fairley 2004). To address this, further evaluation is needed to assess the role's impact and value within
the healthcare system.
In 2004, Guest et al developed an impact framework for nurse consultants to evaluate their effect on service
delivery and patient care. The framework emphasised the indirect impact on patient care through system
development and staff culture. While the study found strong support for consultant-level practice, it also highlighted
significant gaps about understanding role implementation. Guest’s framework for consultant-level practice in
England does not address context specific issues and has not been updated to reflect current working practices.
The focus of the framework is solely on impact with no attention given to role implementation. It is known that
inconsistent governance approaches to implementation can lead to confusion regarding roles and expectations,
hindering workforce planning at scale (Taylor et al 2022). Whilst there is widespread acknowledgement that
consultant-level practice roles have the capacity to impact on the experiences of both patients and frontline staff
(Kennedy et al 2012), the evidence for assessing impact remains unclear.
UEC-specific Consultant Practitioner roles
A focused literature review on consultant-level practice roles in UEC highlighted their contribution to better patient
outcomes through advanced clinical judgment and experience (Dawood & Crouch 2018). In 2017, it was
suggested that CP roles could enhance healthcare access by strengthening the UEC workforce, though conditions
for successful implementation, impact, and receptivity need further exploration (Woo et al 2017). A prior survey
concluded diverse preparation for CP roles often leads to unclear organisational frameworks, with mismatched
expectations and poor preparedness contributing to high staff turnover post-recruitment (Charters et al 2005).
While some evidence has been found regarding UEC CP roles, including to suggest the benefits of consultant-
level practice roles in this setting, little is known regarding barriers and enablers to implementation, role
preparedness, and stakeholder perspectives across micro, meso and macro levels. A recent grant-funded study
(ConNur-ED 2024) showed that less than 1/3 of EDs had implemented such a role and that there is wide variability
regarding role function but did not explore the reasons for this (Mummery & Jarman 2024).
Rising attendances and diverse care models are increasing workloads, impacting recruitment and retention in
UEC, and highlighting the need to expand CP roles (RCEM 2015). There is a clear need to develop career
pathways for nurses and AHPs that acknowledge clinical expertise, with innovative approaches to staffing for
safer, higher-quality, and more efficient care (RCEM 2015). Research gaps have demonstrated the need to
evaluate the development, alignment, and patient impact of new roles (Woodward et al 2005), with current
evidence indicating that UEC CP roles face challenges in their implementation and evaluation, largely due to a
lack of structured approach (Rosser et al 2017, McSherry et al 2007, Parker et al 2011). There is need for a
workforce capable of leading system-level change and delivering expert care across professional domains (Crouch
et al 2024), with sound understanding regarding healthcare providers' and service users' perceptions of CPs and
their recognition of this practice level.
This research will draw on literature within and beyond UEC settings to address gaps and inform the proposed
methodology.
What will this study add to benefit service-users:
New perspectives will be offered on how CPs affect patient experiences and outcomes and their importance to
patients/service-users in ensuring population healthcare needs are met. Identified barriers by service-users and
stakeholders will inform strategies in a proposed co-produced implementation and impact toolkit for UEC settings.
This study will inform workforce policies, national agendas, and gaining professional body support. It will provide
policymakers with insights into effective strategies, ensuring the successful introduction and implementation of CP
roles by identifying and addressing potential barriers.
This project will build on a UK-wide RCEM funded survey undertaken by the applicant and supervisor, highlighting
the lack of established CP roles and significant variability. This will improve care by providing evidence and
recommendations on the implementation and impact of CP roles in UEC by:
Understanding the contexts, mechanisms, and barriers/enablers to successful CP role implementation

 Developing a toolkit that can be used at local, regional, and national levels, to measure the patient-
centered impact of consultant-practice roles
 Reduce variation by supporting equitable access to CPs in UK UEC settings, ensuring patient benefits
across all four domains
Research Question
‘What are understood to be the experiences and perceived barriers/enablers to role transition,
implementation, and impact of multi-professional consultant-level practice within UK UEC settings to co-
produce an implementation and impact toolkit?’
This research aims to address the identified evidence gap by exploring diverse perspectives on CP roles in UEC
settings. It will identify barriers/enablers to role transition, preparedness, implementation and impact for current
CPs and directly/indirectly involved stakeholders at micro/meso/macro level, focusing on professional
expectations, motivators, KPIs, outcomes, and factors critical to successful implementation. Stakeholder
consultation is key to developing and evaluating new models (Parker et al 2011). This study will capture service-
users' perceptions of CP roles in UEC, exploring their impact, value, and potential to improve healthcare access.
Research will adopt an exploratory approach, emphasising qualitative methodology, to uncover perspectives and
provide comprehensive descriptions of stakeholder lived experiences through questioning (Stevenson et al 2011,
Mann et al 2023).
Aims and Objectives
To co-produce a toolkit for introduction, implementation and impact of CPs in UEC settings ready for acceptability
testing.
This study will:
1. Review current literature surrounding consultant-level practice roles through qualitative systematic with meta-
synthesis
2. Explore experiences and perceptions of current CPs in UK UEC settings of their role transition, preparedness,
implementation, impact and effectiveness
3. Determine patient/service-user experiences and perceptions of CP roles in UK UEC settings, priorities for
measuring impact, effectiveness, and evaluation
4. Explore macro and meso-level stakeholder perspectives of the CP role in UK UEC settings to identify
implementation, impact and effectiveness metrics
Project Plan and Methods
This project was developed with the UEC national CP network and key stakeholders, including co-authors of the
NHSE multi-professional consultant-level framework (2023) who provided feedback and revisions. Support has
also been given by the RSS and a patient engagement group. A competitively funded RCEM grant provided
preparation for this project in determining current uptake of CP roles in UK EDs.
This study comprises 4 inter-related work-packages (WP).
WP1: Systematic Review (Objective 1)
A qualitative systematic review with meta-synthesis will explore literature on CP implementation, impact,
effectiveness, evaluation, prevalence, and existing frameworks, led by the applicant with supervisor support as a
second reviewer. Time will be dedicated to refining the review protocol, including formulating review questions
using the FINER principle, designing the search strategy, and defining inclusion/exclusion criteria based on the
PCC (participants, concept, context) approach (JBI online).
Quality appraisal will use CASP, with meta-synthesis managed via a synthesis matrix. PRISMA (2020) will guide
the process, and Rayyan software will assist screening. These tools ensure reliability, minimise bias, and promote
reproducibility (JMLA 2018). The protocol will be published as an output.
The systematic review will inform co-development of interview and focus group schedules (WP2/WP3). Whilst at
this stage the final topic guide has not yet been determined, as this will be informed from WP1, through work
undertaken to date potential themes may include training/transition, role support and understanding of role for
example.
WP2: Interviews (Objectives 1-4)
WP2 and WP3 comprises two complimentary studies across macro (policy), meso (organisational), micro
(individual practitioner) levels. It is important to capture micro, meso and macro levels to address what matters to
those providing and those experiencing health services and the outcomes this brings.
Exploratory study utilising semi-structured interviews with:
1. Current UEC CPs investigating role implementation, impact, barriers/enablers, insights valuable for role
transition including intrinsic/extrinsic motivational factors
2. Key stakeholders, including ICB/ICS employees, chief nurses/AHPs, directors of nursing/AHP, and UEC clinical
directors from trusts with and without CP roles, to explore barriers/enablers, insights for role implementation,
impact evaluation, organisational support readiness
WP3: Focus Groups (Objectives 1-4)
Exploratory study utilising focus groups with:
1. Patients/service-users
2. Consultant Practitioners
Focus groups allow in-depth exploration and understanding of actual, expected, and potential contributions of roles
from multiple perspectives (Parker et al 2011). These will explore implementation barriers/enablers and impact
evaluation metrics, focusing on clinician and patient/service-user perceptions linked to national frameworks. To
minimise biases and ensure reliability, strategies will include a reflexive approach to critically reflect on
preconceptions, relationship dynamics, and analytic focus (Cancelliere et al 2020, Galdas 2017).
Sample and recruitment
Purposive and snowball sampling will be used to select key informants for semi-structured interviews, capturing
diverse perspectives and promoting rich, relevant data (Carlsen & Glenton 2011, Vasileiou et al 2018).
Recruitment will be done via email through networks and advertised through NHS PPIE groups for service-users
and stakeholders. Participants will be invited to contact the lead researcher for consent after receiving an
information sheet. Recruitment aims for a nationally representative sample. The sampling approach will be
regularly reviewed for broad representation. Interviews and focus groups will be offered in person, remotely, or
hybrid.
Recruitment will target micro, meso, and macro levels, including:
• Macro: ICB/ICS regional representatives involved in UEC, professional regulatory bodies, policymakers
• Meso: Executive-level roles in NHS trusts with type-1 (EDs) and type-3 (UTCs or equivalent) departments,
including chief nurses/AHPs, UEC directors of nursing/AHP/medicine
• Micro: UEC CPs, patients/service-users
Sample of approximately 30 interviews is suggested to reach theoretical saturation using a semi-structured
interview approach (Vasileiou et al 2018). Data collection will continue until thematic saturation is reached, where
new data no longer yields new insights, and themes become repetitive. This iterative process combines sampling,
data collection, and analysis until all aspects of the phenomenon are explored, supporting emerging theory
(Hennink et al 2019). The study aims to recruit 30 participants across the UK for one-to-one interviews, as outlined
in the SOECAT.
Literature by Hennink et al (2019) and Carlsen & Glenton (2011) suggest 2-6 focus groups are sufficient to cover
90% of themes. With 4-12 participants per group, smaller groups provide more in-depth insights. Transcripts will
be reviewed after each session to refine the focus for subsequent groups. This study aims to recruit 16 participants
for two focus groups, as outlined in the SOECAT.
This approach ensures a comprehensive exploration of stakeholder experiences, providing rich, triangulated data.
The macro-level examines broader political, economic, and social influences, while the meso-level focuses on
organisational factors shaping change. The micro-level looks at how policy, organisational, and professional
factors intersect to affect CPs' implementation and impact in UEC settings (Ong et al 2014).
Data collection and analysis
In WP2 and WP3, the applicant and PAG will develop interview and focus group questions based on the
systematic review. Interviews and focus groups will be recorded, transcribed, anonymised, and thematically
analysed.
Thematic analysis (Braun & Clarke 2022) will identify patterns in implementation data, with phases including
transcript analysis, theme development, and category confirmation (McSherry et al 2007). Impact data will be
analysed using framework analysis (Gale et al 2013, Smith & Firth 2011), mapped to Guest et al's (2004) work.
This existing framework (Guest et al 2004) will be used, refined and expanded where necessary to assess the
impact of CP roles on patient care and outcomes, including direct/indirect effects. The SRQR checklist will guide
methods and ensure research quality and transparency (EQUATOR online). Emerging themes will inform WP4.
NVivo software will be used to analyse qualitative data from both work packages to support coding and large
datasets management, alongside use of a qualitative data analysis flowchart (Wong 2008). Categories/coded data
will be scrutinised and discussed to ensure accuracy from transcripts. A reflexive process will be undertaken
during data collection and analysis to minimise unconscious bias.
WP4: Co-Produced Toolkit
One collaborative workshop will be held. Workshop recruitment will target 12-15 participants, including CPs,
stakeholders, and service-users, through UK-wide NHS PPIE groups, snowball sampling from interviews, and
existing networks.
Co-design and collaboration with stakeholders will integrate key principles of improvement and participation. Matrix
of framework concepts can arise from literature, interviews and focus groups. Triangulating findings from WP1,
WP2, and WP3 will inform a workshop to develop an implementation and impact toolkit for UEC settings.
Theoretical framework and NVivo will support data triangulation. Stakeholder feedback will assess credibility,
transferability and acceptability prior to testing. Realist evaluation principles will guide toolkit development,
focusing on context, mechanisms, and outcomes (Jack 2022). This approach enhances design rigor and informs
policy and practice (Giles et al 2014).
Toolkit production will follow 3-steps:
1. Introduction – Assessing the needs of service-users, healthcare providers, and the health system to clarify
how the role can meet these needs and ensure stakeholder alignment.
2. Implementation – Ensure policies, resources, and support are in place to introduce the role effectively,
improving understanding of its impact on patients, providers, organisations, and outcomes, to promote
optimal utilisation and achieve expected results.
3. Sustainability – Ensure long-term benefits and impact by identifying ongoing developments, trends, and
needs for role adaptation and support to meet the healthcare system's evolving requirements.
This will:
 Include factors influencing role uptake to increase understanding on those that require attention across
micro, meso and macro levels.
 Support future practice and role development by evaluating implementation and impact, offering strategies
to overcome barriers, and providing a transferable evidence base for effective commissioning and delivery
of CP roles in UEC settings.
 Sustain the workforce, enhance quality, improve health outcomes, promote leadership, and ensure long-
term cost-effectiveness of UEC CP roles.
Enable national evaluation of CP role implementation and impact in UEC settings, identifying key

conditions for success at micro, meso, and macro levels.
Impact, Outputs and Dissemination
This study aims to inform workforce planning in UEC services by collaborating with NHS colleagues and
incorporating service-user perspectives. It will provide evidence to guide decision-makers on CP role
implementation, career progression, retention, and improving service quality and outcomes. This study will identify
enablers and barriers to implementing CP roles in UEC settings from the perspectives of CPs, service-users,
employers, and workforce strategists, contributing to the literature on the need for consultant-level practice in UEC
and benefiting patients/service-users.
Developing robust tools should include inter-professional and service-user engagement to ensure cost-
effectiveness and quality assurance (McSherry et al 2007, NHSE 2017). An evaluative tool can support capturing
the complexity, clinical significance, and impact of CP roles on service-users and professionals focusing on safety,
effectiveness, patient-centeredness, timeliness, efficiency, and equity. Little is known about service-user
perceptions of CPs or recognition of this practice level, which this study will add to the current literature base.
Understanding this could optimise role utilisation, enhance healthcare contributions, and support employers
strategically.
The NIHR impact planning template will guide year three impact/dissemination. Impact will be discussed through
PAG meetings, bulletins, and supervisor updates.
Intended outcomes
 Identification of emerging and contemporary concepts to enhance Guest et al's (2004) framework
 A toolkit for introduction, implementation and impact that compliments the multi-professional consultant-
level practice framework, ready for acceptability testing through future funding applications to assess
transferability across UEC services including:
Identification of barriers and enablers to workforce transformation and effective implementation for UEC CP roles
with potential solutions
Serving as a self-assessment toolkit for role implementation
Providing a coordinated approach to measuring and evidencing the value, benefits, and outcomes of UEC CP
roles through effective implementation
 Increased recognition of the CP role and its impact on quality, safety and effectiveness
 Support for future sustainable workforce planning in the NHS at consultant-practice level. We anticipate
this toolkit will be transferable to other areas and will be available under an open license agreement for
further validation and use
Research findings will be shared locally and nationally with service-users, aspiring CPs, employers, ICBs/trusts,
and key stakeholders, including policymakers, commissioners, and professional bodies.
Dissemination will utilise diverse methods, with PAG members, to ensure wide reach:
 Conference abstracts, posters, and presentations at local, regional, and national levels
 At least 3 peer-reviewed journal publications
Ongoing collaboration with workshop stakeholders, collaborators, and fellowship contacts

toolkit

APPLICATION DETAILS.Patient & Public Involvement:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
Patient demand for UEC services is rising annually, while recruitment and retention of senior clinicians remains a
challenge. In preparation for this application, this ongoing challenge was discussed at a local patient engagement
meeting, emphasising its importance to patients/service-users framing this project proposal.
My experience in PPIE and successfully integrating this into previous projects has been valuable for this study.
Patient/service-user involvement is key to understanding CP roles (NHSE 2017). During application and project
plan development, members from a local patient engagement group were engaged to discuss and present initial
research ideas. This engagement led to helping refine the aims and objectives. This was undertaken by mind
mapping the different elements of the project to capture the intended outcomes and proposed impacts.
Local patient engagement group members included adult service-users and a carer, all with lived experience of
UEC care at a London hospital, including care from a consultant nurse or consultant practitioner in the ED.
Ensuring equal representation in the group is crucial. The group was socially and ethnically diverse, reflecting the
local population to this NHS trust. Feedback from the group confirmed the project's relevance and potential benefit
to those accessing care.
During my pre-doctoral bridging fellowship, I had the opportunity to discuss my project during the taught PPIE
session. The project plan has been discussed with the RSS, through one-to-one sessions, and the plain English
summary was developed with RSS PPIE members and discussed with the NHS trust’s patient engagement group
members. Through discussions with group members, I was able to greater understand how members would like to
be involved in the proposed aspects of this fellowship proposal and came away from these discussions with the
confirmation of willingness from the members to be involved at all stages of the research cycle.

panel member for the NIHR RfPB Under-represented disciplines Nurses and Midwives. He is currently mentoring a

revisions. Professor Crouch will join scheduled supervision sessions with Dr Ahmad and Professor Jarman
providing subject expertise. Professor Crouch will support with proposed research visits to NHSE Centre for
Advancing Practice through his role as clinical lead for consultant-level practice.

Dr Baker is an experienced consultant nurse working at King’s College Hospital ED and holds an honorary Senior

Major Trauma System and is an associate editor for International Emergency Nursing Journal.
Having collaborated with Dr Baker as a co-applicant on the ConNur-ED study, we have already established a

through the continuum from advancing to consultant-level practice within this setting.

line with the four practice pillars, networking and supporting access to opportunities for wider experiences
regionally and nationally.
Collaborations
Emergency Nurse/Practitioner Consultant Group UK
This professional network links together the consultant nurses and consultant practitioners working in urgent and
emergency care settings to share best practice and provide expertise consultancy on emergency care in the UK.
Four members of this group have provided feedback on the project proposal and have agreed to continue to
support the project / act as stakeholders if successful. The group will be key to the success of this work both as
key participants and informants, but also in peer review, feedback and dissemination.
Professor Kim Manley
Professor Manley’s expertise is in practice based interdisciplinary collaborative research that integrates research,
practice and learning. She has a particular focus on leadership development, learning and improvement, system
and workforce transformation to develop and sustain workplace cultures that are person centered, safe, effective
and good places to work. Her expertise aligns with my topic and research subject interests. She was a co-
producer of the centre for advancing practice consultant-level framework. Professor Manley has supported the
development of this research proposal and agreed to provide continued support and collaboration throughout the
fellowship, including supporting through links to the Centre for Advancing Practice consultant-level practice group
and as an expert in research in this field.
Professor Guest and co-authors of 'Guest et al's Consultant Nurse impact framework (2004)':
Professor Guest is Professor at King's Business School. His research and writing address the relationship
between human resource management, organisational performance and employee well-being, the changing role
of careers and quality of working life. Communication has been held on utilisation of Professor Guest et al’s
published impact framework (2004) that aims to be built upon as an outcome of this study and being a point of
contact for expert advice as required.
Translational and Clinical Research Institute (TACRI)
A collaboration between St George’s NHS Trust and City St George’s University, TACRI supports the development
of research collaborations, interests, skills and career progression. TACRI will be a point of contact and support by
providing educational and clinical seminars and access to an expert consultancy service, where I will be able to
sense-check methodologies and data analysis approaches. Additionally, as an existing member I would like to
gain experience in greater involvement in the institute for my future clinical-academic career developing the
research leadership skills required.
Dr Mary Halter
Dr Mary Halter is an applied health services researcher, working in Kingston University’s Emergency
Cardiovascular and Critical Care Research Group. She has collaborated on and led research associated with the
NHS, its workforce and service users including SkillMix-ED; a NIHR funded study which I am an expert panel
member for. Having built a working relationship with Dr Halter through prior work, her expertise links closely with
my research topic and interests for my future clinical-academic career. Dr Halter will support extending my health
services research experience and knowledge and, translating my learning gained from SkillMix-ED study design
into my own research.
Advanced Physiotherapy Practitioner Network (APPN)
APPN supports advanced practice roles across all sectors and specialties of physiotherapy practice in the UK. In
conjunction with my practice supervisor, Dr Noblet, who is an executive member of this network APPN will provide
peer support during both my clinical and academic training plan with opportunity to network and collaborate with
others in extended scope physiotherapy roles across the UK for my professional development. APPN has links
with national bodies supporting planned visits within my training and development plan for me to gain wider
understanding on UK regulation and impact/influence on a strategic level.

Statement of support
We confirm as senior managers at St. George’s University Hospital NHS Foundation Trust (SGUHFT) and City St
George’s, University of London, that we are fully supportive of Victoria’s proposed research and application for an
NIHR Doctoral Clinical Academic Fellowship.
As NHS host institution and clinical partner, SGUHFT is one of London’s biggest hospital Trusts, providing
services to 3.5 million people with a staff of over 9000. The Trust is committed to promoting equality, diversity and
inclusion which is represented in the Trust values and through the NHS workforce disability equality standards.
The senior leadership team in the Trust recognise the critical role that the allied health professions have in
expanding research capacity, patient involvement, patient outcomes and experience. The Trust has developed a
sustainable programme of training and research to increase this research capacity, literacy and capability; to this
end the Trust research strategy 2023-2028 makes specific reference to investing in staff wishing to pursue clinical
academic careers by funding training on research skills and methods, and in providing protected time for
research.
The Translational and Clinical Research Institute (TACRI) hosted by St. George’s University Hospitals NHS
Foundation Trust with City St George’s supports researchers at all levels engage in research and grow our
academic and clinical partnerships. We have an established track record of supporting clinicians who wish to
become research active. Regular research seminars showcase research by Trust staff and research training is
provided through bespoke workshops which would support Vicky in her Fellowship e.g. patient and public
involvement in research, funding opportunities. The Trust has a doctoral and post-doctoral research forum of
which Vicky will be part and which exposes her to a wider network of allied health professionals who are also who
are also developing and succeeding in clinical academic careers. Under this strategy the number of successful
health and care professions research fellowships has grown with current NIHR PCAFs (x5), DCAFs (x4), Senior
Practitioner Award (x1) and ACAF (x1) in the Trust.
Under the guidance of Dr Ahmad, this DCAF will be supported by the Centre for Healthcare Innovation Research
(CHIR) within the School of Health & Medical Sciences (SHMS) at City St George’s, a multi-disciplinary school with
research institutes in Population Health Research, Infection & Immunity, Cardiovascular Sciences & Genomics,
Neurosciences & Cell Biology, and research centres in Healthcare Innovation, Mental Health, Maternal & Child
Health, Language & Communication Sciences, Applied Visual Sciences, and Food Policy, and Clinical, Social &
Cognitive Neuroscience. In the 2021 REF assessment period, in the Allied Health Professions & Nursing unit of
assessment (UoA3), our researchers secured funding from 89 funding bodies (e.g., NIHR, UKRI, Dunhill Medical
Trust, Wellcome Trust) totalling £15.6 million and produced more than 2100 research outputs. 91% of our UoA3
research outputs were rated as world-leading (4*) or internationally excellent (3*), 100% of our submission rated
as outstanding (4*) or very considerable (3*) for impact, and 100% of our research environment ranked 4* or 3*.
CHIR is well placed to support the underpinning research and co-design of a toolkit supporting the implementation
and impact of workforce roles, hosting considerable expertise in behaviour change and complex intervention
development, study design and workforce re-design, complemented by experts in statistics in the Population
Health Research Institute. Victoria will have access to regular research centre meetings where centre members
present research ideas in development, critically discuss and learn from each other. Doctoral researchers are an
important part of this group and have regular opportunities to present and receive feedback on their research.
Activities provided by the centre include training and career development workshops, facilitated writing days,
student presentation days, and access to methodological experts. Victoria will also have access to a regular series
of Early Career Research development workshops, and a dedicated area for research students.
City St George’s is fully committed to Equality, Diversity and Inclusion employment practices, as emphasized in
our 2030 Strategic Vision and overseen by the Dean for Equality, Diversity and Inclusion. We are committed to the
VITAE Concordat to support the career development of researchers and to the principles of the European HR
Excellence In Research Award (HREIRA). City St George’s has held an Athena Swan Silver Award since 2018
and is a Stonewall Diversity Champion and Disability Confident Employer.
This application aligns with the university’s 2030 Strategic Vision, which includes conducting research at the
frontiers of practice; influencing the health policy agenda in the UK and globally; increasing the involvement of the
public and patients and use of co-design in our research activities; supporting our academics to develop their
research profile, through recognition by external bodies and stakeholder groups; and focusing on delivering
research with high quality impact. City St George’s has both ARC South London and ARC North Thames
membership which further facilitates co-production of research through patient and public involvement in research.
Victoria has demonstrated a long-standing commitment to academic practice and was awarded a competitive
Trust-funded Research Fellowship in 2023 in recognition of the strength of her potential as a future academic
leader. She used the time awarded on the Fellowship, in addition to the NHSE NIHR pre-doctoral bridging
scheme, to develop her DCAF application and expand her research skills and knowledge through conference
presentations and being a Principal Investigator on a NIHR-funded study.
The Trust has an agreed pathway for the development of clinical academic posts at pre- and post-doctoral level,
further supported by the George’s Academic Training (GAT) programme, and in the medium term we aim to
support Victoria beyond a successful DCAF and PhD by developing a specific clinical academic role for her to
apply for on completion of this fellowship. The emergency department collaborative research group team in which
Victoria is based has a strong multi-professional academic track record with two NIHR academic clinical fellows
working alongside a Professor of Emergency Care. She will continue in her practice role for 20% of her time on the
fellowship and remain fully integrated within the clinical team.
